segments.text: The next Suta is 7.6.53.
Once, when the Exalted One dwelt near Rajagaha
on Mount Voucher Peak, two devas of surpassing loveliness
lighting up almost the whole peak approached the Exalted One,
at the passing of the night, saluted Him and stood at one side.
And standing there, one deva said,
Lord, these nuns are freed, and the other said,
Lord, these nuns are wholly freed and without attached remainder.
Thus spoke those devas and the master approved,
and seeing that they saluted the Exalted One
and keeping Him on their right, vanished from there.
I'll just stop here for a moment to commend.
You'll notice, even though the Buddha had psychic power,
quite often the devas have only deems.
They would come and inform the Buddha about certain things
that were happening around, and that is because,
even though the Buddha has psychic power,
he did not make any effort to know what was going around him,
because usually he'd abide in meditation, he abide in Jhana,
and he is being a person who is liberated,
he doesn't worry about the world,
but sometimes he does worry about his disciples.
Then he'll go and give advice to his disciples sometimes.
Then the Sutta continues.
At dawn, the Exalted One told the monks all that had taken place.
Now at that time, the Venerable Mahamobelana
sat near the Exalted One, and he thought,
what devas know the partly attached as partly attached,
the wholly detached as wholly detached.
About then, a monk named Tissa had recently died,
and had arisen in one of the Brahma worlds,
and there they knew him as Brahmatissa, the mighty, the very powerful.
And the Venerable Mahamobelana, as a strong man,
might stretch forth his bent arm, or bend his outstretched arm,
vanished from Mount Baalchopee, and appeared in that Brahma world.
And Brahmatissa saw the Venerable One from afar coming along,
and on seeing him said, come worthy Mogulana,
welcome worthy Mogulana.
It's been long indeed since you made this round,
that is to say, came here, be seated worthy sir,
here on this seat made ready.
And the Venerable Mahamobelana sat down on the seat made ready,
and Brahmatissa, after paying homage to him,
also sat down at one side.
And the Venerable Mahamobelana said to Brahmatissa,
thus seated, to what devas, Tissa, is there this knowledge,
as to being partly unattached, he is there,
as to being wholly unattached, he is there.
And the Brahmatissa said,
the Brahmadeva hosts worthy Mogulana have this knowledge.
What have they all this knowledge?
No worthy sir, those devas who are satisfied with Brahma life,
Brahma beauty, Brahma happiness, Brahma power,
and no, not the escape from it, to the beyond, as there really is,
they have not this knowledge, as to being partly unattached, he is there,
as to being wholly unattached, he is there.
But those devas of the Brahma hosts who are not so satisfied,
and no, they escape to the beyond, they have this knowledge.
Consider worthy Mogulana, the man who is freed both ways,
of him those devas know thus, this reverend sir is freed both ways,
and so long as his body lasts, devas and men shall see him,
but on the breaking up of the body, they shall not see him,
and thus it is worthy sir, that these devas have the knowledge,
as to being unattached, he is there.
I'll just stop here to commend.
So you see this Mahamogulana, the Arahana,
went to consult the Brahmatissa.
It might be that this deva was formerly a disciple of the
Venerable Mahamogulana, that is why the Venerable Mahamogulana
seems to be very badly with him, went to consult him,
and the Brahmatissa paid respect to the Venerable Mahamogulana.
And in the previous Sutta, Angutani Kaya 6.4.34,
we also read about how Venerable Mahamogulana consulted this same Brahmatissa,
about how many devas have entered the stream.
Now here the Brahmatissa is talking about the first type of Arya,
the both ways liberated.
This feed both ways, or both ways liberated,
refers to the Arahana, who is liberated by mind and liberated by wisdom,
who is also called and Arahana liberated by mind.
Then he continues,
again consider him who is liberated by wisdom,
they know of him the same.
Consider the man who is a body witness, Kaya Sakhi,
of him they know, this reverence is a body witness,
perhaps by practicing things proper as to sleeping and sitting,
having fellowship with good friends, controlling the faculties,
he may enter and abide in that consummation beyond all of the holy life,
realising it here and now by his own knowledge,
for the sake of which clansmen rightly go forth from the home to the homeless life.
And thus it is worthy, sir, that these devas have the knowledge,
as of being partly unattached, he is that.
Consider him who is a view-winner or view-attainer, Nithipato,
and then the faith released, Sada Vimutto,
and then the Dhamma follower, Dhamma Nussarin,
of each they know, this reverence is such a one,
and perhaps by practicing things proper,
they may enter and abide in the consummation beyond all of the holy life,
and thus these devas know, as to being partly unattached, he is that.
I'll stop here again to commend.
The first two, the free both ways, or liberated both ways,
and liberated by mine refers to the arahand,
and they are wholly unattached, but the others mentioned here,
they are Arya who have not completed their work,
and because of that they are partly unattached,
not wholly unattached.
According to the Angutra Nikaya 3.3.21,
the body-witness, then the view-attainer,
and the faith-released, either Sakadagamin, Anagamin,
or 4th path Arya, Arhataya Patipano,
the 2nd fruition, 3rd fruition, or the 4th path Arya.
And the Sutta continues,
and the Venerable Mahamogalana, delighted, pleased by Brahmatisa's words,
as a strong man might bend his arm to and fro,
vanish from the Brahma world, and appeared on Mount Vulture peak,
and he approached the exalted one, saluted him, and sat down at one side,
and seated thus the Venerable Mahamogalana,
related to the exalted one, all that talk that he had with Brahmatisa,
and the Buddha said,
but Brahmatisa did not teach you the 7th person, Mughalana,
the dweller in the signless, and the Venerable Mughalana said,
now is the time, O blessed one, now is the time, O welfareer,
were the exalted one to teach concerning the dweller in the signless,
the monks would hear and remember,
therefore Mughalana, listen, pay heed, I will speak.
His Lord rejoined the Venerable Mahamogalana,
and the exalted one said,
consider Mughalana, the monk who, by inattention to all signs,
enters and abides in signless mental concentration,
of him those devas know,
this reverence abides in signless mental concentration,
perhaps by practicing things proper as to sleeping and sitting,
having fellowship with good friends and control of the faculties,
he may enter and abide in that consummation beyond all of the holy life,
realising it here, now, by his own knowledge,
for the sake of which clansmen rightly go forth from the home to the homeless life,
and thus it is Mughalana that these devas have the knowledge,
as to being partly unattached, he is that, as the end of the sutta.
Here, the Brahmatisar mentioned about the six types of Arya,
he left out, the last one not mentioned is the Sada Nusarin,
the faith follower, he did not mention,
but the Dhamma Nusarin, the Dhamma follower,
and the Sada Nusarin, the faith follower,
are first path attainers,
according to the Sawhitna Nikaya, 25.1,
and then the Buddha talked in the end about the one who abides
in signless mental concentration,
signless mental concentration is that concentration of mind,
which is not attached to any sign,
which does not have any sign, any object in the mind.
Now, the next sutta is 7.6.54.
Once, while the exalted one dwelt near Vaisali,
at the Gable Hall in Mahavana,
General Sihar visited him, saluted and sat down at one side,
so seated he said to the exalted one,
is it possible, Lord, to show the visible result of giving?
Well, Sihar, just on that I will question you in return,
explain the matter as it seems good to you.
What do you think, Sihar, there might be two men,
one unbelieving, mean, Maisali, cross grain,
the other a believer, a master giver,
delighting in constant giving.
What do you think, Sihar,
on whom would compassionate arahants first have compassion?
The unbelieving, mean, Maisali, cross grain man,
or the believer, the master giver,
who delights in constant arms giving?
And he said, the unbeliever, Lord,
why should they first have compassion on him?
But the believer, on him surely they would first have compassion.
Then what do you think, Sihar,
which of the two would visiting arahants first visit?
The believer surely, Lord.
From whom would receiving arahants first receive arms?
From the believer surely, Lord.
To whom would teaching arahants first teach dhamma?
To the believer surely, Lord.
Or of whom would a fair report be brought,
be noise abroad?
That means a good report, be circulated.
Of the believer surely, Lord.
Who would approach any gathering,
nobles, brahmin, householders, recluses,
with confidence, untroubled and unashamed?
And then, Sihar said, the believer surely, Lord.
And what do you think, Sihar,
who on the breaking up of the body after death
would arise in the happy heaven world?
The unbelieving, mean, Maisali, cross grain man,
or the believer, the master giver,
who delights in constant arms giving?
The unbeliever, Lord,
why shall he on the breaking up of the body
after death arise in the happy heaven world?
But the believer after death may arise
in the happy heaven world.
Lord, these six visible results of giving
declared by the exalted one.
I go not to the exalted one by faith for that.
I just know them.
I, Lord, am a giver, a master giver.
And on me, compassionate arahants,
first have compassion.
Me, they first visit.
From me, they first receive.
Me, they first teach dhamma.
Of me, a fair report is noise abroad.
General Sihar is a giver, a worker,
the Sangha servant.
It is said,
any gathering I approach,
nobles, Brahmins, householders, recluses,
I approach with confidence, untroubled.
Lord, these six visible results of giving
declared by the exalted one.
I go not to the exalted one by faith for that.
I just know them.
But when the exalted one said,
the giver Sihar, the master giver,
on the breaking up of the body after death
arises in the happy heaven world,
then I know not.
And there I go to the exalted one by faith.
And the Buddha said,
does it is Sihar, does it is?
The giver Sihar, the master giver,
on the breaking up of the body after death
arises in the happy heaven world.
That's the end of the Sutta.
So in this Sutta, the Buddha has described
the visible results of giving.
And General Sihar said that
the first six results he can see
to be true because it is applicable to him
because he's a great giver,
a great person who makes a lot of offerings
to the monks.
But the last one he said that he could not see
and that a giver by arms giving
would take rebirth in the heaven world.
So he said he just has to believe the Buddha
for that this General Sihar used to be
one of the followers of the external psychosatics.
But later he went to see the Buddha
and was converted by the Buddha.
And in one of the suttas later will come across
he invited the Buddha to his house for food
and he served the Buddha with meat.
And the external psychosatics were jealous
that they had lost such a famous disciple,
such a powerful disciple.
And they spread around the world
that the Buddha was eating meat
that was purposely slaughtered for the Buddha.
And the Buddha, when the Buddha heard that
then the Buddha informed the people
that they only eat meat,
the Buddha is disciples,
only eat meat with three conditions.
That they did not slaughter the animal
or ask somebody to slaughter.
Second, they don't see the killing.
Thirdly, they don't hear the animal cry
as it was being slaughtered.
And this shows that the Buddha
and his disciples did eat meat.
Only that it had these three conditions.
Now they come to Sutta 7.6.56.
Once when the exalted one
was staying in the bamboo grove near Kimbila,
the venerable Kimbila approached him,
saluted and sat down at one side.
So seated he said,
Lord, what is the cause, the reason why
when the Tathagata has become completely cool,
saddhamma or true dhamma will not last?
And the Buddha said,
suppose Kimbila,
when the Tathagata has become completely cool,
the monks, nuns, laymen and laywomen
live without reverence, without heed for the teacher,
live without reverence, without heed for dhamma,
live without reverence, without heed for the sangha,
live without reverence, without heed for the training,
without reverence, without heed for concentration,
live without reverence, without heed for earnestness,
live without reverence, without heed for goodwill,
verily Kimbila, this is the cause,
the reason why when the Tathāgātā has become completely cool,
saddhamma or true dhamma will not last as the end of the sutta.
This is one of those suttas where the courses for the disappearance of the true dhamma,
that means the discourses of the Buddha are given.
And here we find that it's because people have no respect for the Buddha.
Instead of following the Buddha, they prefer to follow some or other.
And then they have no reverence for the dhamma,
that means the discourses of the Buddha.
Because in the Angutra Gikaya 4.180 the Buddha said that dhamma refers to the suttas, the discourses.
And then the Sangha is the Bikku Sangha here.
It's not referring to the Arya Sangha because you don't know who is an Arya, who is not an Arya,
but you know who is a monk, who is not a monk.
So here means without reverence for the Sangha of monks and possibly because the monks do not follow the precepts.
People lose faith, lose respect for the monks and slowly.
That also causes the downfall of the true dhamma.
The training means keeping the precepts generally.
And then for concentration, no reverence for concentration.
This we can see nowadays when people say that samadhi, which the Buddha refers to as the janas,
or one pointedness of mind in the suttas, they say it's not necessary.
And then without reverence for good will, good will or metha is necessary if Buddhists are to be united and in harmony.
Then the next sutta is 7.6.57.
Monks holding fast to seven things.
A monk may destroy the asavas and enter and abide in the asava-free emancipation by mind.
What seven?
Here in monks, a monk is full of faith, virtuous, learned, lives secluded, has sustained energy, is mindful, is wise.
Purely monks holding fast to these seven things.
A monk may destroy the asavas and enter and abide in the asava-free emancipation by mind.
At the end of the sutta, here are mentioned certain qualities, certain states,
which are very important for a monk to attain destruction of the asavas.
The first one is having faith sada in the buddhadama and sangha.
The second one is virtuous, that means he has moral conduct, keeps the precepts.
Third one, he's learned, that means he's knowledgeable on the suttas and the vinaya.
The fourth one, he lives secluded.
Seclusion is very important for a monk who wants to strive to end suffering,
and then after that he has sustained energy or energetic effort,
and then he's mindful, practices sattva, and finally he is wise, he has wisdom.
The next sutta is 7.6.58.
Once, while the exalted one dwelt among the bagis on Crocodile Hill in the Deer Park at Besakala Grove,
the venerable Mahamogalana sat nodding in the village of Kala Vaalamutta among the Magadis,
and the exalted one with the deva eye, surpassing man's in clearness, saw him seated there nodding,
and as a strong man might bend his arm to and fro, the exalted one vanished from the Deer Park
and appeared before the venerable Mahamogalana, and the exalted one sat down on the appointed seat,
and when he was seated, he said to the venerable Mahamogalana,
you were nodding Mogalana, you were dozing Mogalana.
Yes Lord, replied, venerable Mahamogalana, I'll just stop here for a while.
This is one of very interesting and important suttas, and this is a time when venerable Mahamogalana
had just become a disciple of the Buddha not long, and he had not yet become an arahan,
so he was practising very hard, and according to some other later books for seven days
after the venerable Mahamogalana became a disciple of the Buddha, he did not sleep,
he was practising so hard that he tried not to sleep at night,
and after seven days he attained arahanhood, so this sutta doesn't mention how many days
that was after the venerable Mahamogalana had joined the Buddha, and then the Buddha said,
accordingly Mogalana, if while you abide thoughtful, the thought comes, drowsiness has descended,
take no heed of it, make no ado of that thought, and maybe as you abide so, that drowsiness will pass.
If abiding so, it pass not, then you should ponder in your heart on dhamma as heard, as mastered,
exploit with your mind, review it, and maybe as you abide so, that drowsiness will pass.
If it pass not, then you should repeat dhamma in detail, as heard, as mastered,
and maybe that drowsiness will pass.
If it pass not, then you should pull both earlobes and need the limbs with the hand,
and maybe that drowsiness will pass.
If it pass not, then you should arise from sitting, cleanse the eyes with water,
survey the horizon, and gaze up at the starry constellations,
and maybe that drowsiness will pass.
If it pass not, then you should apply your mind to the thought of light, fix your mind on the thought of day,
as by day, so by night, as by night, so by day.
Thus with mind unhindered, unhampered, you should make your thought become radiant,
and maybe that drowsiness will pass.
If it pass not, then with the senses withdrawn, the mind not outward gone,
you should fix your thought on the meditation walk, conscious of it to and fro,
and maybe that drowsiness will pass.
If abiding so, that drowsiness pass not, then you should lie down on your right side,
foot covering foot, mindful, aware, with mindset on the thought of arising,
and on awakening Mogalana, get up quickly, thinking,
I'll not live yoke to the pleasures of lying, reclining, and drowsiness.
Verily, let your training be thus Mogalana.
Moreover Mogalana, let your training be thus,
not lifted up with pride, will I visit families.
Verily Mogalana, let your training be thus,
for in families Mogalana, there are many things to be done,
whereby the men may not notice the monks coming,
and if the monk visit lifted up with pride, he'll think,
who now I wonder has embroiled me with this family,
these men have now no liking for me,
and so from getting nothing, he becomes troubled,
he becomes worked up, being worked up, he becomes uncontrolled.
When uncontrolled, the mind is far from concentration.
Moreover Mogalana, let your training be thus,
I'll talk no provoking talk.
Verily Mogalana, let your training be thus,
where there is provoking thought Mogalana,
much talk may be expected,
and there is much talk, one becomes worked up,
being worked up, one becomes uncontrolled.
When uncontrolled, the mind is far from concentration.
Mogalana, I praise not fellowship just with all,
nor do I not praise such fellowship.
Indeed Mogalana, I praise not fellowship with house dwellers,
with wanderers, with dwellings that have,
but dwellings that have few sounds, little noise,
are free from folk's breath,
where one may live apart from man in befitting solitude,
it's such that I praise.
Now when he had thus spoken,
the Verily Mogalana said this to the exalted one,
Briefly Lord, how becomes a monk freed by cravings destruction,
reaching the perpetual peace from effort,
the perpetual holy state, the perpetual finality,
and becomes foremost among devas and men.
And the Buddha said,
Here in Mogalana, a monk has learned this,
it befits not to be engrossed in the things of this world.
If Mogalana, a monk has learned this,
it befits not to be engrossed in the things of this world.
He recognizes each condition, or each state, dhamma,
recognizing each condition,
he understands each condition,
understanding each condition,
whatever feeling he feels, pleasant, painful, or neither,
he abides seeing the impermanence of those feelings,
viewing them dispassionately, looking for their end,
regarding them as something to be renounced.
Living in such contemplation,
he cleaves not to anything in the world,
not cleaving to anything, he craves not,
not craving for anything,
he becomes just completely cool in himself,
and he knows birth is destroyed,
lived is the holy life, done is what had to be done,
there is no more of this to come.
Barely Mogalana, in this manner briefly,
a monk becomes freed by cravings destruction,
reaching the perpetual end,
the perpetual peace from effort,
the perpetual holy state,
the perpetual finality,
and becomes foremost among devas and men,
at the end of the sutta.
It's quite an interesting and important sutta.
Here, the Buddha found the Venerable Mahamogala dosing,
so he gave some very practical advice,
he told the Venerable Mahamogala,
he said, now, if dousiness comes,
firstly take no heed of it,
just try to ignore that dousiness,
but if you can't ignore that dousiness,
then think about dhamma,
review dhamma that you have heard,
maybe the dousiness will pass,
but if it doesn't,
then you should repeat dhamma in detail.
You remember in the Buddha's days,
there were no books,
so they had to repeat dhamma
after they had learned sutta,
so repeat it.
So here the Buddha is saying,
the third thing to do is to repeat dhamma,
and then if by repeating the dhamma,
the dousiness still doesn't go away,
then the Buddha said,
pull both ears,
and rub the limbs with the hands,
massage the limbs with the hands,
maybe to get the blood flowing,
and maybe dousiness will go away,
but if it does not,
then clean the eyes with water,
and look up into the sky,
look at the stars, etc.,
maybe the dousiness will pass,
but if it does not again,
then think of light,
make the mind think of light,
so that when the mind is bright,
it doesn't become sleepy,
but if that doesn't work,
then the Buddha said,
take a walk, take a meditation walk,
be mindful and take a meditation walk,
and if even by walking,
the dousiness doesn't go away,
then finally the Buddha said,
lie on the right side,
take a rest,
but get up quickly,
as soon as one wakes up,
then get up quickly,
don't lie on the bed,
most people tend to lie on the bed,
and enjoy the pleasure of lying down,
but the Buddha said, don't enjoy it,
so these are the practical steps
the Buddha gave,
then after that the Buddha gave him
another three types of advice,
the first one, not to have pride
when he visits families,
because the Buddha said,
sometimes when a person,
when a monk goes to visit families
on arms around,
sometimes they might not notice him,
they have many things to do,
they might not notice him,
and might not come out to bring him food,
and he waits outside the door,
doesn't get any food,
if he has pride, he might think,
these people are purposely not giving him food,
and then he becomes worked up,
so the Buddha said, not to have any pride,
the second one, the Buddha said,
not to talk, any provoking talk,
then there will be a lot of talk,
and then not too much talk,
one becomes unmindful,
and the mind doesn't become concentrated,
and thirdly the advice the Buddha gave
was to live apart in seclusion,
but this generally,
not all monks can do that,
live apart, live in seclusion,
because you need a lot of discipline,
a monk doesn't have enough training and discipline,
then instead of practicing more,
he might tend to sleep more,
then the Buddha,
then the Venerable Mahamogala
asked the Buddha for advice
on how to attain the final goal,
and the Buddha said,
to remember that one should not be engrossed,
should not be engrossed with the things of the world,
and then if one is not engrossed
in the things of the world,
then one abides seeing the impermanence of feelings,
viewing them dispassionately,
and then one cleaves not to anything in the world,
and from that one does not crave for anything in the world,
and one lets go, renounces everything in the world,
and slowly one becomes completely cool.
